video_title: How the Dollar Quietly Took Over the World feat. Brendan Greeley | Global Macro | Ep.104
gist: Financial historian Brendan Greeley argues the dollar's global dominance rests not on US military might or a fragile network effect but on three concrete pillars: uniquely deep federally-insured deposits, Fed swap lines, and New York/London capital-market plumbing. He frames the Eurodollar market (~$14T offshore vs ~$19T domestic) as the still-growing, hard-to-replace core of dollar hegemony, and criticizes the Fed for treating QE and a large balance sheet as politically safe defaults that mainly inflate asset prices rather than effective tools.

themes.theme: Big Money vs. Small Money Framework

insights.claim: Small-denomination money is structurally more expensive to produce per unit of value than large-denomination money (small coins needed the same labor-intensive hammer strike as big ones), so mints historically preferred big coins and modern banks likewise prefer high-value depositors, borrowers and transfers.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Explains persistent underbanking as a cost-of-production feature, not a policy accident, pointing to structurally underserved small-borrower and payments niches.
insights.quote: It's just expensive to underwrite a small loan as it is to underwrite a large loan. You got the same back office costs no matter what.

insights.claim: Because banks avoid small loans and small deposits, the US is uniquely bad at financial inclusion, with many families lacking bank accounts at all despite a large community-bank network.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Highlights a durable addressable market for fintech/low-finance products serving the unbanked that incumbent banks structurally decline to serve.

insights.claim: When official money fails, ordinary people repeatedly invent their own functional money at the local level (hard-times money in 19th-century US crises, city-issued dollar notes during the Depression), regardless of what policymakers do internationally.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Suggests grassroots/private money creation (today: stablecoins, local credit) reliably fills gaps and should be watched as a leading indicator of monetary stress.
insights.quote: people will make up their own money at their own level to work no matter what you do at the large international policy level

insights.claim: Payments is treated as a low-status 'backwater' at the Fed, and the US check format is essentially unchanged since a 1792 check drawn on the Bank of North America, reflecting chronic neglect of everyday payment plumbing.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Points to persistent modernization gaps in US payments that faster-rails and fintech entrants can exploit.
insights.quote: Payments is a backwater at the Fed. It's the assignment you get when you don't get to do the big fun important macho macro stuff.

insights.claim: The United States did not create its own currency but adopted an existing global one, a Spanish silver coin (piece of eight) minted from Mexican and Bolivian silver bearing a German-derived name, meaning monetary sovereignty must be fought for and built rather than assumed at founding.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Reframes reserve-currency status as a slowly-built institutional achievement, implying dollar succession would require decades of plumbing, not a single political decision.
insights.quote: sovereignty is overrated or rather it's very important but we tend to assume sovereignty when in fact sovereign monetary sovereignty has to be fought for

insights.claim: The dollar originated as a high-value silver dividend coin (the Joachimsthaler) minted in a Bohemian valley to pay Saxon mining investors, whose large volume made it spread as merchant settlement money across Europe long before it became a national currency.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Illustrates that dominant international money originates as high-powered money for investors/merchants, not retail cash, a template relevant to any dollar-successor debate.

insights.claim: Even Holy Roman Emperor Charles V could not control silver markets and had to mint his New World silver as a faithful copy of the already-familiar thaler to sell it through Antwerp into global trade, showing dominant money forces even sovereigns to conform.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Reinforces that network dominance of a money form constrains even the most powerful issuers, a lens for assessing yuan-internationalization prospects.

insights.claim: Cipolla's rule of dominant money holds that there is typically one dominant form and other monies are 'more or less faithful copies' of it, a pattern Greeley applies from medieval coins to the modern dollar.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Provides a mental model for reserve-currency competition: challengers tend to imitate rather than displace the dominant standard.
insights.quote: with big money there tends to be one dominant form of money and a lot of other monies are what he called more or less faithful copies of that dominant money

insights.claim: The Mexican silver dollar remained legal circulating money in America until the 1850s and dollar monetary sovereignty was not fully complete until 1932, underscoring how gradual the transition from foreign silver to domestic bank money was.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Sets a multi-decade timescale for how long full currency transitions actually take, tempering fast dollar-collapse narratives.

insights.claim: There are roughly $14 trillion in offshore dollars versus roughly $19 trillion in domestic dollars, meaning the external Eurodollar market is nearly as large as the entire US domestic money supply.
insights.speaker: Alan Dunn (host)
insights.so_what: The offshore dollar market is too large and deep to be sidelined quickly, so dollar-demise theses must explain why this pool keeps growing.
insights.quote: there's 14 trillion dollars in offshore dollars and 19 trillion dollars in domestic dollars

insights.claim: London banks began creating brand-new dollar-denominated loans and deposits by marking up their own ledgers, generating dollars entirely outside the reach of the US Treasury or Fed, a phenomenon the 'fiat money' framework cannot explain.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Private offshore dollar creation means dollar supply and dominance are not fully controllable by US authorities, a structural feature markets often underweight.
insights.quote: banks were marking up their own ledgers with brand new loans, brand new deposits denominated in dollars, and they were doing this in London

insights.claim: Offshore dollar (Eurodollar) volumes continue to grow, including within China over recent years, so the dominant form of dollars in the world is expanding rather than shrinking despite talk of de-dollarization.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: De-dollarization narratives are contradicted by rising offshore dollar usage even in China; watch Eurodollar growth as the real dominance gauge.
insights.quote: So when we worry about the loss of dollar dominance, we have to look at the dominant form of dollars in the world. It's euro dollars. Um and man that doesn't seem to be decreasing.

insights.claim: Global trade settlement requires short-term dollar bank loans to finance inventory in transit, an entrenched institutional habit built over 50 years that is very difficult to imagine being replaced.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Trade-finance dependence on dollar credit is a deep moat for dollar dominance, raising the bar for any rival currency to displace it.

insights.claim: Treating money as simply 'fiat' (the state declares it money) makes analysts incurious about how money is actually produced, obscuring important dynamics like private offshore dollar creation.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Analysts who model money endogenously (bank credit creation) gain edge over those relying on the simplified fiat story.
insights.quote: when we learn that money is fiat uh it makes us incurious about how it's actually produced

themes.theme: The Three Pillars Anchoring the Dollar

insights.claim: The dollar's international value rests on three concrete pillars, not a fragile network effect: a uniquely vast pool of federally-insured deposits (no eurozone-wide deposit insurance exists), Fed swap lines, and general US institutional quality.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Frames dollar risk assessment around three measurable/observable factors rather than sentiment, making deposit-insurance and swap-line integrity the key things to monitor.
insights.quote: There are three things that anchor the value and the use of the dollar internationally right now.

insights.claim: Stablecoins are effectively a form of uninsured bank dollar, and Greeley expects a crash in stablecoin value followed by tighter regulation forcing providers to pay for deposit insurance.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Flags stablecoins as a fragility point and predicts a regulatory tightening cycle, relevant to crypto and payment-token exposure.
insights.quote: I think the more likely outcome is that we'll get a crash in stable coin value and um, everybody and they'll be brought into the regulatory fold a little more tightly.

insights.claim: The accumulated know-how of New York and London capital markets to turn almost any asset into a dollar-denominated investment is itself a pillar of dollar dominance that could outlast the United States, much as the Bank of England outlasted the British Empire.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Suggests dollar-asset issuance infrastructure is stickier than US political power, and a credible yuan rival would need a comparable financial center.

insights.claim: The dollar should not be read as a referendum on the United States: foreign attitudes toward America have soured markedly, yet users keep 'gritting their teeth' and transacting in dollars for rational financial reasons.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Political anti-Americanism is a poor short signal for dollar demand; the financial plumbing dominates, so fade dollar-collapse trades driven by sentiment.
insights.quote: we can't think of the dollar as a referendum on the United States. Like, people are mad at the United States, but they're still gritting their teeth and using the dollar.

themes.theme: Politicization of Swap Lines and Fragmentation

insights.claim: Fed dollar swap lines have historically carried no political strings, extended to any responsible central bank, but Greeley warns they could become politicized under Kevin Warsh, forcing countries to eventually choose between accepting the Fed's strings or the PBOC's.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Politicized swap lines would fragment the global dollar safety net and accelerate bloc formation, a tail risk for EM funding stability.
insights.quote: countries may need to decide in the future whose strings do I prefer, the PBOC's or the Fed strings if indeed uh the the swap lines become politicized

insights.claim: The US Treasury operates its own swap lines separate from the Fed's, and Scott Bessent has signaled willingness to use them for explicitly political purposes, dangling them before Argentina to encourage America-friendly policies.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Treasury swap-line conditionality turns dollar liquidity into a geopolitical tool, a factor for pricing EM sovereign and currency risk.
insights.quote: Scott Besson has made very clear that he's going to use those swap lines for political purposes. he's going to dangle them uh for Argentina

insights.claim: The People's Bank of China offers yuan-denominated swap lines but at far smaller volume than the Fed's, and recipient countries have learned those lines come with strings attached.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Yuan swap infrastructure remains a weak substitute for dollar liquidity, limiting near-term yuan internationalization.

themes.theme: The Fed as a Bank: QE and the Balance Sheet

insights.claim: Central bank policy is fundamentally about adding or removing items from the balance sheet, a flexible tool, yet the Fed's near-total refusal to buy anything other than treasuries and agency securities is a 'failure of imagination' rather than a legal constraint.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Implies large untapped policy optionality (buying other assets) that could reshape markets if political/philosophical barriers fall.
insights.quote: the utter reluctance by the Fed to ever contemplate buying anything other than treasuries is a profound failure of imagination

insights.claim: QE mainly raises the value of existing assets, making wealthy asset-holders wealthier, and Greeley is not convinced it does anything else; the Fed favors it because buying treasuries feels politically safe rather than because it is effective.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: If QE is mostly an asset-price and distributional lever rather than a real-economy tool, its future use and unwinding matter more for asset valuations than for growth.
insights.quote: I think that QE is a default policy um that doesn't really work that well. Uh it basically it pushes up the value of existing assets. That's all it does. It makes wealthy people wealthier.

insights.claim: During the financial crisis the Fed had legal authority to buy and renegotiate mortgages one by one (effective but administratively tedious) but chose to buy treasuries instead to avoid political trouble, contributing to mass foreclosures and lasting economic scarring.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Reveals a Fed bias toward politically safe over effective interventions, useful for predicting how it acts in the next crisis.

insights.claim: Congress in the 1970s gave the Fed a mandate to grow monetary and credit aggregates in ways that increase productivity, but the Fed has quietly abandoned it because that task requires politically fraught discretion over which credit is productive.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Signals that the Fed's self-defined mandate is narrower than its legal one, leaving room for Congress to redirect it toward credit allocation.
insights.quote: the Fed makes up its own homework, uh grades itself on its own homework, and then shows up at Congress twice a year and says, "Look, didn't we do a good job?"

insights.claim: The Fed's balance sheet stays large mainly out of habit and is hard to shrink once banks grow accustomed to ample reserves, making Kevin Warsh's questions about whether the US is permanently stuck in an ample-reserves regime legitimate.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Balance-sheet inertia implies limited scope for meaningful QT, keeping structural liquidity elevated for markets.
insights.quote: Why can't we ever shrink it uh ever again? Um are we stuck forever with the ample reserves regime?

insights.claim: Central banks are simply banks chartered for specific missions that legitimately change over time (Bank of England to fund war against France; the Fed bought federal war debt to fight Germany), so Congress can and arguably should redefine the Fed's job, even toward financing a green transition.
insights.speaker: Brendan Greeley (guest, author/historian)
insights.so_what: Anticipates a possible expansion of central-bank mandates (e.g., green finance), which would broaden the range of assets the Fed might buy.
insights.quote: none of this was the job of a central bank. We had to invent central banks to do jobs that we needed them to do and then we change those jobs periodically
